--- title: "Financial Structure, Patent Protection, and the Quality–Quantity Tradeoff in Innovation" subtitle: "Replication Appendix" author: Aaron DiLorenzo date: today format: html: linkcolor: '#74a7d8' theme: default page-layout: article toc: true toc-depth: 1 toc-location: right number-sections: true code-fold: true code-summary: "Show code" code-link: true code-overflow: scroll code-line-numbers: true code-copy: true include-in-header: text: | execute: enabled: true python: false echo: true eval: true warning: false message: true cache: true freeze: auto number-sections: true self-contained: true editor: markdown: wrap: 72 --- --- ### Resources & Data Access * **[Download/View the Full Thesis (PDF)](documents/analysis.pdf)** * **[Download the Replication Source Code (.qmd)](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/diloaaron/diloaaron.github.io/refs/heads/main/thesis.qmd)** * **[Access the Raw and Final Datasets](https://github.com/diloaaron/diloaaron.github.io/tree/main/data)** * **[Request OECD Patent Quality Data](https://forms.office.com/pages/responsepage.aspx?id=1MdBrGEfDUaw9PySWitHHKuxmuqpz_9KusL7-G1D6wFUOEU0OVBYVk5QTzROVlBTSUtBUUREWVhHTiQlQCN0PWcu&route=shorturl)** *(Required for the initial data construction phase)* --- # Patent Quality Indicators Construction The dependent variables for patent quality are derived from the OECD Patent Quality Indicators database (2025 edition). While the database provides a wide array of metrics (forward citations, family size, and grant lag), this thesis focuses on two specific measures: Composite Quality Index (`quality_index_4`): An unweighted average of four normalized components (forward citations, patent family size, radicalness, and number of technical fields). Breakthrough Patents (`breakthrough`): A binary indicator identifying patents in the top 1% of the citation distribution within their respective technology-year cohort. Because the OECD quality indicators are provided at the patent-application level without geographical identifiers, a multi-stage merging procedure is required to construct the country-year panel used in the analysis. The Quality Indicators file is joined with the OECD HAN (Harmonised Applicant Names) database using the unique application ID (`appln_id`). To assign each patent to a country, the HAN database is linked to the Harmonised Names (HARM_NAMES) database, allowing me to map each application to the `Person_ctry_code` (ISO country code) of the patent applicant. Patents are assigned to years based on their filing date. Finally, the patent-level data is aggregated to the country-year level. The `quality_index_4` is averaged across all patents for a given country-year to create a mean quality score, while the breakthrough indicator is summed to calculate the total count of high-impact patents. The following Python script (using the `polars` library for high-performance data manipulation) executes this pipeline: ::: {.cell} ```{.python .cell-code} """ OECD Patent Quality Indicators normalization, winsorization, and aggregation procedures """ import polars as pl import os OUTPUT_DIR = "" os.makedirs(OUTPUT_DIR, exist_ok=True) # for reproducing, change the file paths to correctly the downloaded data, # they're too large for me to make this self running INDICATORS_FILE = "Data/202502_OECD_PATENT_QUALITY_EPO_INDIC.txt" COHORTS_FILE = "Data/202502_OECD_PATENT_QUALITY_EPO_INDIC_COHORT.txt" HAN_PATENTS_FILE = "Data/202502_HAN_PATENTS.txt" HARM_NAMES_FILE = "Data/202502_HARM_NAMES.txt" FINAL_PATENT_FILE = "OECD_patent_quality_final.parquet" FINAL_COUNTRY_FILE = "OECD_country_year_final.csv" WINSOR_VARS = ["family_size", "grant_lag", "bwd_cits", "npl_cits", "claims", "fwd_cits5", "fwd_cits7"] COMPOSITE_VARS = ["generality", "originality", "radicalness", "quality_index_4", "quality_index_6"] # load the data def load_data(): cohorts = pl.read_csv(COHORTS_FILE, separator="|", infer_schema_length=0) cohorts = cohorts.rename({c: c.strip() for c in cohorts.columns}) indicators = pl.read_csv(INDICATORS_FILE, separator="|", infer_schema_length=10000) indicators = indicators.rename({c: c.strip() for c in indicators.columns}) han = pl.read_csv(HAN_PATENTS_FILE, separator="|") han = han.rename({c: c.strip() for c in han.columns}) han = han.rename({"Appln_id": "appln_id"}) harm = pl.read_csv(HARM_NAMES_FILE, separator="|") harm = harm.rename({c: c.strip() for c in harm.columns}) harm = harm.select(["HARM_ID", "Person_ctry_code"]) han = han.join(harm, on="HARM_ID", how="left") return cohorts, indicators, han # main function def main(): cohorts, indicators, han = load_data() merged = indicators.join(han, on="appln_id", how="left") # ensure breakthrough is binary if "breakthrough" in merged.columns: merged = merged.with_columns( pl.col("breakthrough").fill_null(0).cast(pl.Int8) ) # aggregation to country/year agg_exprs = [] # normalized indicators norm_cols = [c for c in merged.columns if c.endswith("_norm")] for c in norm_cols: agg_exprs.append(pl.mean(c).alias(f"avg_{c}")) # mean for composite indicator for c in COMPOSITE_VARS: if c in merged.columns: agg_exprs.append(pl.mean(c).alias(f"avg_{c}")) # breakthrough counts agg_exprs.extend([ pl.sum("breakthrough").alias("breakthrough_count"), pl.count("appln_id").alias("patent_count"), (pl.sum("breakthrough") / pl.count("appln_id")).alias("pct_breakthrough") ]) country_agg = ( merged .filter(pl.col("Person_ctry_code").is_not_null()) .group_by(["Person_ctry_code", "filing"]) .agg(agg_exprs) .rename({"Person_ctry_code": "iso3c", "filing": "year"}) .sort(["iso3c", "year"]) ) country_agg.write_csv( os.path.join(OUTPUT_DIR, FINAL_COUNTRY_FILE) ) merged.write_parquet( os.path.join(OUTPUT_DIR, FINAL_PATENT_FILE) ) if __name__ == "__main__": main() ``` ::: # Data Construction and Variable Assembly In this section, I assemble the final panel dataset by merging the OECD Patent Quality Indicators with macroeconomic and financial development data. ### Dependent Variables I construct two primary dependent variables: 1. **Patent Quality (`quality_index_100`)**: The raw OECD composite quality index is scaled to a 0-100 range for ease of interpretation. 2. **Patent Quantity (`ln_patents_pc`)**: patenting intensity is measured as patents per capita (scaled per million residents). This is then $\log(x+1)$ transformed to account for right-skewness and potential zero values. ### Independent Variables & Mean-Centering To accurately capture the interaction between financial structure and intellectual property rights, I apply a $\log(x+1)$ transformation to our financial development variables (Bank Credit, Market Capitalization, and Venture Capital as a % of GDP) due to their right-skewness. **Both** the Park Index (IPR) and the logged Financial Development variables are mean-centered *prior* to interaction. This ensures that the main effect coefficients in the regressions represent the marginal effect evaluated at the sample mean, significantly improving the interpretability of the final models. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(data.table) library(dplyr) options(dplyr.summarise.inform = FALSE) # 1. load individual datasets pqi <- fread("data/OECD_country_year_final.csv") fd <- fread("data/FD_Final.csv") vc <- fread("data/VC_final.csv") counts <- fread("data/counts_res_final.csv") controls <- fread("data/Controls_final.csv") park <- fread("data/Park_final.csv") # 2. clean up and standardize missing values clean_missing <- function(df) { df[df == ".."] <- NA df[df == "NA"] <- NA df[df == ""] <- NA return(df) } pqi <- clean_missing(pqi) fd <- clean_missing(fd) vc <- clean_missing(vc) counts <- clean_missing(counts) controls <- clean_missing(controls) park <- clean_missing(park) # Remove old 'researchers' column to prevent any errors counts <- counts %>% select(-researchers_per_million) # 3. merge individual datasets into a master panel df <- pqi %>% left_join(park %>% select(iso3c, year, ipp_index_IP_transformed), by = c("iso3c", "year")) %>% left_join(fd, by = c("iso3c", "year")) %>% left_join(vc, by = c("iso3c", "year")) %>% left_join(counts, by = c("iso3c", "year")) %>% left_join(controls, by = c("iso3c", "year")) # 4. ensure correct numeric typing numeric_vars <- c( "population_total", "gdp_pc_const2015usd", "bank_credit_gdp", "market_cap_gdp", "VC_investment", "trade_percent_gdp", "rd_expenditure_percent_gdp", "tertiary_enrollment_percent", "patent_applications_residents", "breakthrough_count", "patent_count", "ipp_index_IP_transformed" ) for (v in numeric_vars) { if (v %in% names(df)) { df[[v]] <- as.numeric(df[[v]]) } } # 5. Variable Construction and Transformations df <- df %>% mutate( # dependent variable: Patents per capita (scaled per million residents) patents_per_capita = (patent_applications_residents / population_total) * 1000000, patents_per_capita = ifelse( is.infinite(patents_per_capita) | patents_per_capita < 0, NA, patents_per_capita ), # dependent variable: Scaled Patent Quality quality_index_100 = avg_quality_index_4 * 100, # rescale VC to match the percentage format of other financial variables VC_investment_pct = VC_investment * 100, # log transformations (log(x + 1)) ln_patents_pc = log(patents_per_capita + 1), ln_gdp_pc = log(gdp_pc_const2015usd + 1), ln_bank = log(bank_credit_gdp + 1), ln_market = log(market_cap_gdp + 1), ln_vc = log(VC_investment_pct + 1), ln_trade = log(trade_percent_gdp + 1), ln_rd = log(rd_expenditure_percent_gdp + 1), ln_tertiary = log(tertiary_enrollment_percent + 1), # mean-centering prior to interaction ipr_c = ipp_index_IP_transformed - mean(ipp_index_IP_transformed, na.rm = TRUE), ln_bank_c = ln_bank - mean(ln_bank, na.rm = TRUE), ln_market_c = ln_market - mean(ln_market, na.rm = TRUE), ln_vc_c = ln_vc - mean(ln_vc, na.rm = TRUE), # interaction terms ln_bank_x_ipr = ln_bank_c * ipr_c, ln_market_x_ipr = ln_market_c * ipr_c, ln_vc_x_ipr = ln_vc_c * ipr_c ) %>% # drop unneeded intermediate columns select( -avg_generality, -avg_originality, -avg_radicalness, -`Country Name.x`, -FS.AST.PRVT.GD.ZS, -CM.MKT.LCAP.GD.ZS, -`Country Name.y` ) ``` ::: # Baseline Two-Way Fixed Effects (TWFE) Models In this section, I estimate two-way fixed effects (TWFE) panel regressions to establish baseline relationships between financial structure, intellectual property rights, and my two distinct innovation outcomes: Patent Quality and Patent Quantity. ### Model Specification and Methodological Choices For each of the three financial development channels (Market Capitalization, Bank Credit, and Venture Capital), I estimate models sequentially introducing macroeconomic controls. The baseline equation takes the form: $$Y_{it} = \beta_1 FD_{it}^c + \beta_2 IPR_{it}^c + \beta_3 (FD_{it}^c \times IPR_{it}^c) + \mathbf{X'}_{it}\beta + \alpha_i + \lambda_t + \epsilon_{it}$$ **Features of this approach:** 1. **Centered Variables (**$^c$): As established in the data preparation phase, both the financial development variable ($FD$) and the Park Index ($IPR$) are mean-centered. This ensures that $\beta_1$ and $\beta_2$ are interpreted as the marginal effects of finance and IPR exactly at the sample mean of the other variable, rather than at an unrealistic zero value. 2. **Fixed Effects (**$\alpha_i, \lambda_t$): I include country fixed effects to absorb time-invariant institutional or cultural characteristics, and year fixed effects to control for global macro shocks and aggregate patenting trends. 3. **Clustered Standard Errors**: To account for serial correlation within countries over time, all standard errors are clustered at the country level using `vcovHC`. ### Estimation and HTML Tables The following custom function estimates the incremental models and outputs clean HTML tables using `stargazer`. Note that the dataset `df` is carried over directly from the previous chunk's environment. ```{.r .cell-code} library(plm) library(lmtest) library(sandwich) library(stargazer) # function to calculate country-clustered standard errors cluster_se <- function(model) { sqrt(diag(vcovHC(model, type = "HC1", cluster = "group"))) } # master function to run TWFE models and output HTML tables run_twfe_incremental <- function(depvar, fd, fd_x_ipr, title) { # filter missing values specific to the target variables to maximize sample size base <- df %>% filter( !is.na(.data[[depvar]]), !is.na(.data[[fd]]), !is.na(ipr_c) ) # convert to panel dataframe panel <- pdata.frame(base, index = c("iso3c", "year")) # define sequential formulas f1 <- as.formula(paste(depvar, "~", fd, "+ ipr_c +", fd_x_ipr)) f2 <- update(f1, . ~ . + ln_gdp_pc) f3 <- update(f2, . ~ . + ln_trade) f4 <- update(f3, . ~ . + ln_rd) f5 <- update(f4, . ~ . + ln_tertiary) # estimate within-country, two-way models m1 <- plm(f1, data = panel, model = "within", effect = "twoways") m2 <- plm(f2, data = panel, model = "within", effect = "twoways") m3 <- plm(f3, data = panel, model = "within", effect = "twoways") m4 <- plm(f4, data = panel, model = "within", effect = "twoways") m5 <- plm(f5, data = panel, model = "within", effect = "twoways") # format labels fin_label <- switch(fd, "ln_market_c" = "Log Market Cap (Centered)", "ln_bank_c" = "Log Bank Credit (Centered)", "ln_vc_c" = "Log Venture Capital (Centered)" ) # output formatted HTML table for this document html_table <- capture.output( stargazer( m1, m2, m3, m4, m5, type = "html", title = title, dep.var.caption = ifelse( depvar == "quality_index_100", "Dependent Variable: Patent Quality (0–100)", "Dependent Variable: Log Patents per Capita" ), covariate.labels = c( fin_label, "IPR Index (Centered)", paste(fin_label, "x IPR"), "Log GDP per Capita", "Log Trade Openness", "Log R&D Expenditure", "Log Tertiary Enrollment" ), se = list( cluster_se(m1), cluster_se(m2), cluster_se(m3), cluster_se(m4), cluster_se(m5) ), omit.stat = c("ser", "f"), notes = c( "Country and year fixed effects included in all models.", "Standard errors clustered by country." ), notes.append = TRUE, no.space = TRUE, header = FALSE ) ) # print HTML table to the Quarto render cat(paste(html_table, collapse = "\n")) cat("

") # Add spacing between tables invisible(list(m1, m2, m3, m4, m5)) } # patent quality models market_quality <- run_twfe_incremental( depvar = "quality_index_100", fd = "ln_market_c", fd_x_ipr = "ln_market_x_ipr", title = "Market Finance and Average Patent Quality" ) ```

# Zero-Inflated Negative Binomial (ZINB) Models In this supplementary analysis, I shift the focus from average patent quality and volume to the extreme right tail of the innovation distribution: **breakthrough patents**. ### Methodological Justification Breakthrough innovations are inherently rare, meaning the `breakthrough_count` variable is strictly non-negative count data characterized by two econometric challenges: 1. **Excess Zeros**: Most country-year observations record zero breakthrough patents. 2. **Overdispersion**: The variance of breakthrough counts significantly exceeds the mean, violating the assumption of standard Poisson regression. To address this, I estimate Zero-Inflated Negative Binomial (ZINB) models. The ZINB framework simultaneously estimates two equations: * **The Selection (Zero-Inflation) Model**: A logistic regression predicting the probability that a country is in a "certain zero" state. * **The Count Model**: A negative binomial regression estimating the actual number of breakthrough patents produced, conditional on the country clearing the zero-hurdle, while explicitly modeling the overdispersion. ### Model Specification Just as in the baseline models, I utilize mean-centered financial development ($FD_{it}^c$) and institutional quality ($IPR_{it}^c$) variables. The models below iterate through the financial channels (Market Capitalization, Bank Credit, and Venture Capital), sequentially adding macroeconomic controls. *(Note: Because ZINB models generate coefficients for both the count and zero-inflation equations simultaneously, the raw model outputs are provided below. Formatted, consolidated tables are available in the main text of the thesis).* ```{.r .cell-code} library(pscl) library(dplyr) library(knitr) # create the offset variable (internal patents from the OECD data, distinct from constructed patents/capita) df$ln_internal_patents <- log(df$patent_count) # function to run ZINB and output clean HTML tables run_zinb_incremental <- function(finance_var, title_name) { # match exactly with the variable names fd_c <- paste0(finance_var, "_c") fd_x <- paste0(finance_var, "_x_ipr") controls <- c("ln_gdp_pc", "ln_trade", "ln_rd", "ln_tertiary") models <- list() # 1. estimate Models for (i in 1:4) { intensity_controls <- paste(controls[1:i], collapse = " + ") formula_str <- paste0( "breakthrough_count ~ ", fd_c, " + ipr_c + ", fd_x, " + ", intensity_controls, " + offset(ln_internal_patents) | ln_gdp_pc" ) models[[i]] <- zeroinfl( as.formula(formula_str), data = df, dist = "negbin" ) } # 2. output HTML Tables cat("##", title_name, "\n\n") for (j in 1:4) { cat("### Model", j, "\n\n") model_sum <- summary(models[[j]]) # calculate Log Theta explicitly for the HTML table m <- models[[j]] if (!is.null(m$theta) && !is.null(m$SE.theta)) { lt_est <- log(m$theta) lt_se <- m$SE.theta / m$theta } else { lt_est <- NA lt_se <- NA } # Count model count_coefs <- model_sum$coefficients$count if (!is.na(lt_est)) { count_coefs <- rbind(count_coefs, "Log Theta" = c(lt_est, lt_se, NA, NA)) } count_table <- data.frame( Variable = rownames(count_coefs), Coefficient = round(count_coefs[,1], 3), Std_Error = round(count_coefs[,2], 3) ) cat("#### Intensity (Negative Binomial Count Equation)\n\n") print(knitr::kable(count_table, row.names = FALSE)) cat("\n\n") # selection model zero_coefs <- that these variables meaningfully impact our innovation outcomes, I perform joint significance (Wald) tests. Specifically, I test the null hypothesis that the main effect and the interaction term are jointly equal to zero ($H_0: \beta_{Main} = \beta_{Interaction} = 0$). ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(car) # custom function to execute and format robust joint significance tests test_joint_significance <- function(model, var_main, var_interaction, test_name) { cat(paste0("====================================================\n")) cat(paste0("Joint Test: ", test_name, "\n")) cat(paste0("H0: ", var_main, " = 0 AND ", var_interaction, " = 0\n")) cat(paste0("====================================================\n")) # execute linear hypothesis test using clustered standard errors wald_result <- linearHypothesis( model, c(paste0(var_main, " = 0"), paste0(var_interaction, " = 0")), vcov. = vcovHC(model, type = "HC1", cluster = "group") ) print(wald_result) cat("\n\n") } # quality joint tests cat("### PATENT QUALITY MODELS ###\n\n") # market Finance Model test_joint_significance(market_quality[[4]], "ln_market_c", "ln_market_x_ipr", "Market Cap & Interaction (Quality)") test_joint_significance(market_quality[[4]], "ipr_c", "ln_market_x_ipr", "IPR & Interaction [Market Model] (Quality)") # replaces plm, lmtest, and sandwich # it was harder to do this with plm so i switched packages options(dplyr.summarise.inform = FALSE) run_trend_incremental <- function(depvar, fd, fd_x_ipr, title) { # maximize samples by finance type base <- df %>% filter( !is.na(.data[[depvar]]), !is.na(.data[[fd]]), !is.na(ipr_c) ) # ensure year is numeric so fixest treats iso3c[year_numeric] as a linear trend base$year_numeric <- as.numeric(as.character(base$year)) # specify the fixed effects and trend part of the formula fe_trend <- "| iso3c + year + iso3c[year_numeric]" # build specifications f1 <- as.formula(paste(depvar, "~", fd, "+ ipr_c +", fd_x_ipr, fe_trend)) f2 <- as.formula(paste(depvar, "~", fd, "+ ipr_c +", fd_x_ipr, "+ ln_gdp_pc", fe_trend)) f3 <- as.formula(paste(depvar, "~", fd, "+ ipr_c +", fd_x_ipr, "+ ln_gdp_pc + ln_trade", fe_trend)) f4 <- as.formula(paste(depvar, "~", fd, "+ ipr_c +", fd_x_ipr, "+ ln_gdp_pc + ln_trade + ln_rd", fe_trend)) f5 <- as.formula(paste(depvar, "~", fd, "+ ipr_c +", fd_x_ipr, "+ ln_gdp_pc + ln_trade + ln_rd + ln_tertiary", fe_trend)) # run models with fixest (cluster = ~iso3c applies HC1 clustered standard errors) m1 <- feols(f1, data = base, cluster = ~iso3c) m2 <- feols(f2, data = base, cluster = ~iso3c) m3 <- feols(f3, data = base, cluster = ~iso3c) m4 <- feols(f4, data = base, cluster = ~iso3c) m5 <- feols(f5, data = base, cluster = ~iso3c) # format custom labels for the table var_dict <- c( quality_index_100 = "Patent Quality (0–100)", ln_patents_pc = "Log Patents per Capita", ln_market_c = "Log Market Cap (Centered)", ln_bank_c = "Log Bank Credit (Centered)", ln_vc_c = "Log Venture Capital (Centered)", ipr_c = "IPR Index (Centered)", ln_market_x_ipr = "Log Market Cap x IPR", ln_bank_x_ipr = "Log Bank Credit x IPR", ln_vc_x_ipr = "Log Venture Capital x IPR", ln_gdp_pc = "Log GDP per Capita", ln_trade = "Log Trade Openness", ln_rd = "Log R&D Expenditure", ln_tertiary = "Log Tertiary Enrollment" ) cat(paste0("### ", title, "\n\n")) table_text <- capture.output( etable( m1, m2, m3, m4, m5, dict = var_dict, signif.code = c("***"=0.01, "**"=0.05, "*"=0.1), fitstat = ~ n + r2 ) ) cat("```text\n") cat(paste(table_text, collapse = "\n")) cat("\n```\n") cat("\n*Notes: Country and year fixed effects included. Country-specific linear time trends included. Standard errors clustered by country.*\n") cat("

\n\n") invisible(list(m1, m2, m3, m4, m5)) } # quality models- market_quality <- run_trend_incremental( depvar = "quality_index_100", fd = "ln_market_c", fd_x_ipr = "ln_market_x_ipr", title = "Market # Variable Glossary **[Source for PQI Data](https://forms.office.com/pages/responsepage.aspx?id=1MdBrGEfDUaw9PySWitHHKuxmuqpz_9KusL7-G1D6wFUOEU0OVBYVk5QTzROVlBTSUtBUUREWVhHTiQlQCN0PWcu&route=shorturl)** **[Source for WDI data](https://databank.worldbank.org/source/world-development-indicators)** **[Source for VC data](https://data-explorer.oecd.org/vis?df[ds]=DisseminateFinalDMZ&df[id]=DSD_VC%40DF_VC_INV&df[ag]=OECD.SDD.TPS&dq=...USD_EXC.A&pd=2007%2C&to[TIME_PERIOD]=false)** **[Source for Park Index](https://edspace.american.edu/waltergpark/2025/09/01/patent-rights-index/)** ### Dependent Variables - `quality_index_100` - The OECD composite patent quality index (`avg_quality_index_4`), scaled to a 0–100 range for interpretability. - `patents_per_capita` - Patent applications by residents, scaled per million people. - `ln_patents_pc` - Log-transformed patenting intensity ($\log(x+1)$ of `patents_per_capita`). This replaces previous researcher-scaled metrics to better capture output while maximizing sample size. - `breakthrough_count` - The absolute count of breakthrough patents (top 1% most cited) produced by a country in a given year. Used exclusively as the dependent variable in the ZINB models. ### Financial Development Variables (Independent) - `bank_credit_gdp` - Domestic credit provided by the financial sector (% of GDP). Proxy for traditional banking depth. - `market_cap_gdp` - Market capitalization of listed domestic companies (% of GDP). Proxy for public equity market depth. - `VC_investment_pct` - Venture capital investment as % of GDP (rescaled by $\times 100$ to match the percentage units of the other financial variables). - `ln_bank`, `ln_market`, `ln_vc` - Log-transformed ($\log(x+1)$) versions of the respective financial metrics to address right-skewness. - `ln_bank_c`, `ln_market_c`, `ln_vc_c` - Mean-centered versions of the log-transformed financial variables. Centering ensures that the main effects in the interaction models are evaluated at the sample mean. ### Institutional Variables (Independent) - `ipp_index_IP_transformed` - The Ginarte-Park Intellectual Property Protection Index (0–5 scale), using a standard five-year fill interpolation for missing inter-period years. - `ipr_c` - Mean-centered Park Index. ### Interaction Terms - `ln_bank_x_ipr`, `ln_market_x_ipr`, `ln_vc_x_ipr` - The interaction between the mean-centered financial variable and the mean-centered Park Index ( `ln_market_c` $\times$ `ipr_c`). ### Macroeconomic Controls *Note: All continuous macro controls are $\log(x+1)$ transformed to capture proportional relationships and minimize the influence of extreme outliers.* - `ln_gdp_pc` - Log of real GDP per capita (constant 2015 USD). - `ln_trade` - Log of trade openness (Imports + Exports as % of GDP). - `ln_rd` - Log of Gross Domestic Expenditure on R&D (% of GDP). - `ln_tertiary` - Log of gross tertiary school enrollment (%).
